blocks.body: In case shows precaution: we do something now because a situation might happen later.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In case does not mean the same as if. It highlights precaution: we prepare for a possible situation in advance.","rules":[{"label":"PREPARE AHEAD","color":"emerald","text":"We do something first because something may happen later.","example":"Take an umbrella in case it rains."},{"label":"AFTER IN CASE","color":"blue","text":"After in case, we usually use the present simple, even when the meaning is future.","example":"I'll write it down in case I forget."},{"label":"NOT THE SAME AS IF","color":"amber","text":"If introduces a condition, while in case explains why we prepare in advance.","example":"Take an umbrella if it rains. / Take an umbrella in case it rains."}]}

{"title":"1. Form","intro":"Three common patterns are easy to recognise:","items":[{"label":"Classic pattern","title":"main clause + in case + present","subtitle":"I'll save the file in case the computer crashes."},{"label":"With past meaning","title":"in case + past","subtitle":"I took some cash in case the card didn't work."},{"label":"With a noun","title":"in case of + noun","subtitle":"In case of fire, use the stairs."},{"label":"Short spoken form","title":"just in case","subtitle":"I'll bring a charger just in case."}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Precaution","color":"emerald","description":"We do something now so that we are ready for a possible later situation.","examples":[{"en":"Take a jacket in case it gets cold later.","ua":"Take a jacket in case it gets cold later."},{"en":"I'll send you the address in case you can't find the place.","ua":"I'll send you the address in case you can't find the place."}]},{"label":"Difference between if and in case","color":"blue","description":"If describes what will happen under a condition. In case explains why we prepare in advance.","examples":[{"en":"If it rains, we'll stay inside.","ua":"If it rains, we'll stay inside."},{"en":"We'll take umbrellas in case it rains.","ua":"We'll take umbrellas in case it rains."}]},{"label":"The phrase just in case","color":"amber","description":"Just in case often appears without an extra clause when the precaution meaning is already clear.","examples":[{"en":"I'll print one more copy, just in case.","ua":"I'll print one more copy, just in case."},{"en":"Put some water in your bag, just in case.","ua":"Put some water in your bag, just in case."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Confusing if and in case.","wrong":"Call me in case you need help.","right":"Call me if you need help.","hint":"There is no preparation in advance here, so the correct linker is if."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using will after in case.","wrong":"Take a coat in case it will get cold.","right":"Take a coat in case it gets cold.","hint":"After in case, we usually keep the present form."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Forgetting the meaning of precaution.","wrong":"I'll stay at home in case it rains.","right":"I'll stay at home if it rains.","hint":"If the action happens only under the condition, you need if, not in case."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["In case = a precaution now for a possible later situation.","After in case, we usually use the present simple, not will.","If introduces a condition, while in case introduces a reason to prepare.","Just in case often works as a short phrase on its own."]}
